Remove the module before welding anything to the implement to prevent damage.
How many hydraulic hoses need to be connected to the tractor?Four hydraulic hoses need to be connected to the tractor: two from the P and T ports on the valve and two for the bypass.
What should be adjusted if the implement moves too quickly or too slowly?Depending on the tractor's age and size, you may need to adjust the flow rate of the hydraulic remotes to achieve the desired speed of implement movement.
How should the wiring harness be connected to the proportional valve?Attach the two plugs from the harness to the connectors on the proportional valve, ensuring correct orientation. The connectors may need to be swapped if manual controls in the app work in reverse.
Where should the large Deutsch connectors on the main harness be plugged into the Control Module?The large Deutsch connectors on the main harness should be plugged into ports A and C of the Ditch Assist Control Module. Ports B and D should be left vacant and sealed with dust plugs.
How should the power harness be connected during installation?The power harness should be linked directly to the tractor battery terminals and connected to the power connector on the main harness. It should only be connected to the vehicle battery, not a trailer plug or in-cab power strip.
How should the GPS breakout cable be routed during installation?The GPS breakout cable should be routed from the open connector on the harness to the GPS antenna location, ensuring proper cable management and protection from potential damage.
What should be done to prevent damage to components during equipment operation?Ensure that all connectors are securely plugged, ports are sealed when not in use, and cables and hoses are properly secured to prevent damage to Ditch Assist components during equipment operation.
Can the Control Module be installed on any suitable spot on the equipment?Yes, the Control Module can be securely installed either at the implement's front, near the hitch, or at the tractor's rear, as long as it provides a clear line of sight to the cab and minimal obstructions.
How should the proportional valve be positioned during installation?The proportional valve should be securely installed in a location that allows the hydraulic hoses to connect to it and enables the attached hoses to reach the tractor's rear ports.
Can the proportional valve be permanently mounted on the tractor?Yes, if you intend to use Ditch Assist with multiple implements, it is suggested to permanently mount the valve on the tractor for convenience.
What precautions should be taken when mounting the valve bracket on a scraper or land leveler?The valve bracket should be securely mounted to the implement, ensuring it won't interfere with moving parts or hydraulic lines. Remove the module before welding anything to the implement to prevent damage.
How many hydraulic hoses need to be connected to the tractor?Four hydraulic hoses need to be connected to the tractor: two from the P and T ports on the valve and two for the bypass.
What should be adjusted if the implement moves too quickly or too slowly?Depending on the tractor's age and size, you may need to adjust the flow rate of the hydraulic remotes to achieve the desired speed of implement movement.
How should the wiring harness be connected to the proportional valve?Attach the two plugs from the harness to the connectors on the proportional valve, ensuring correct orientation.
What is Ditch Assist?Ditch Assist is a compatibility-focused system designed to work with various GPS brands/models.
Which GPS message standards does Ditch Assist support?Ditch Assist supports NMEA 2000 and NMEA 0183 message standards.
What is NMEA 2000?NMEA 2000 is a CAN-based communication protocol used in marine electronics.
What is NMEA 0183?NMEA 0183 is a standard associated with 'serial port' communication in marine electronics.
What are the required NMEA output settings for Ditch Assist?The required NMEA output settings are specified for GPS configuration.
What is the recommended baud rate for NMEA output?The recommended baud rate for NMEA output is 38,400.
At what rate should the GGA message be emitted?The GGA message should be emitted at 10Hz (5Hz if 10Hz is not available).
At what rate should the VTG message be emitted?The VTG message should be emitted at 10Hz (5Hz if 10Hz is not available).
Which messages should be disabled in the NMEA output?All other messages should be disabled in the NMEA output.
What should be done if message rates cannot be specified?If message rates cannot be specified, enable the highest rate for all messages.
Is Ditch Assist compatible with all GPS brands and models?Yes, Ditch Assist is designed to be compatible with a wide range of GPS brands and models.
Can Ditch Assist work with any GPS communication protocol?Yes, Ditch Assist supports both CAN-based (NMEA 2000) and 'serial port' (NMEA 0183) protocols.
What is the purpose of Ditch Assist?The purpose of Ditch Assist is to enhance GPS functionality in various applications.
How does Ditch Assist improve GPS compatibility?Ditch Assist improves GPS compatibility by working with different GPS brands and models.
What is the significance of the NMEA 2000 message standard?The NMEA 2000 message standard allows for communication between marine electronics devices.
Why is NMEA 0183 important in the context of Ditch Assist?NMEA 0183 is important for 'serial port' communication compatibility in Ditch Assist.
What is the recommended baud rate for NMEA output in Ditch Assist?The recommended baud rate for NMEA output in Ditch Assist is 38,400.
How frequently should the GGA message be emitted in Ditch Assist?The GGA message should be emitted at a rate of 10Hz (5Hz if 10Hz is not available) in Ditch Assist.
How frequently should the VTG message be emitted in Ditch Assist?The VTG message should be emitted at a rate of 10Hz (5Hz if 10Hz is not available) in Ditch Assist.
Which messages should be disabled in the NMEA output for Ditch Assist?In the NMEA output for Ditch Assist, all other messages should be disabled.
What should be done if specific message rates cannot be set in Ditch Assist?If specific message rates cannot be set, enable the highest rate for all messages in Ditch Assist.
Is Ditch Assist compatible with GPS brands and models used in marine applications?Yes, Ditch Assist is compatible with GPS brands and models used in marine applications.
Can Ditch Assist work with both CAN-based and 'serial port' communication protocols?Yes, Ditch Assist can work with both CAN-based (NMEA 2000) and 'serial port' (NMEA 0183) communication protocols.
What benefits does Ditch Assist provide for GPS users?Ditch Assist provides improved compatibility, enhanced functionality, and wider GPS support.
How does Ditch Assist ensure compatibility with different GPS brands/models?Ditch Assist is designed with compatibility in mind to ensure it works with a wide range of GPS brands/models.
What are the key features of Ditch Assist?The key features of Ditch Assist include compatibility, support for NMEA standards, and enhanced GPS functionality.
What is Ditch Assist?Ditch Assist is a technology developed to work with a wide range of GPS brands and models.
Which message standards does Ditch Assist support?Ditch Assist supports both the NMEA 2000 and NMEA 0183 message standards.
What is the NMEA 2000 message standard?The NMEA 2000 message standard is a CAN-based communication protocol.
What is the NMEA 0183 message standard?The NMEA 0183 message standard is associated with 'serial port' communication.
What should the GPS be configured to emit for Ditch Assist?The GPS should be configured to emit GGA and VTG messages at a specific frequency, and all other messages should be disabled.
What should the baud rate be set to?The baud rate should be set to 38,400.
What frequency should the GGA message be set at?The GGA message should be set at a frequency of 10Hz, or 5Hz if 10Hz is not available.
What frequency should the VTG message be set at?The VTG message should be set at a frequency of 10Hz, or 5Hz if 10Hz is not available.
Should other messages be enabled or disabled?All other messages should be disabled.
What if you can't designate a specific message rate for each message?If a specific message rate cannot be designated for each message, then the highest rate should be enabled for all messages.
Why does Ditch Assist require specific NMEA output settings?Ditch Assist requires these specific settings to ensure optimal compatibility and performance with various GPS devices.
What are the primary NMEA messages needed for Ditch Assist?The primary NMEA messages needed for Ditch Assist are GGA and VTG.
Can Ditch Assist work with any GPS brand?Yes, Ditch Assist was designed with compatibility in mind and works with a wide range of GPS brands and models.
What type of communication protocol does the NMEA 2000 standard use?The NMEA 2000 standard uses a CAN-based communication protocol.
What type of communication is associated with the NMEA 0183 standard?The NMEA 0183 standard is associated with 'serial port' communication.
Why do the GGA and VTG messages need to be set at a frequency of 10Hz?The 10Hz frequency for GGA and VTG messages ensures a faster update rate, which provides more accurate and responsive control for Ditch Assist.
Is it a problem if 10Hz frequency is not available for the GGA and VTG messages?If 10Hz is not available, a frequency of 5Hz can be used as a fallback for the GGA and VTG messages.
What should you do if you can't disable all other messages?If you can't disable all other messages, the next best option is to enable the highest rate for all messages.
Is it possible to use Ditch Assist with a GPS device that doesn't support the NMEA 2000 or NMEA 0183 standards?While Ditch Assist primarily supports the NMEA 2000 and NMEA 0183 standards, it's designed to be compatible with a wide range of GPS models, so it might work with other standards as well.
Why is the baud rate set to 38,400?A baud rate of 38,400 is commonly used for serial communication because it provides a high data transfer rate, which helps to ensure that the Ditch Assist system can respond rapidly and accurately.
What is the purpose of the GGA message in Ditch Assist?The GGA message provides essential GPS fix data, which Ditch Assist uses for precise navigation and control.
What is the purpose of the VTG message in Ditch Assist?The VTG message provides course and speed information relative to the ground, which Ditch Assist uses for precise navigation and control.
How does Ditch Assist use the NMEA messages from the GPS?Ditch Assist uses the NMEA messages to provide precise navigation and control based on the GPS data.
Is there a particular order in which the GPS settings should be configured for Ditch Assist?There is no specified order, but it is crucial to ensure that the baud rate is set correctly, the GGA and VTG messages are set at the appropriate frequency, and all other messages are disabled.
What is the accuracy of RTK?Real-Time Kinematic (RTK) GPS is a technique used to enhance the precision of position data derived from satellite-based positioning systems. It is capable of providing positioning to within a centimeter or less with the correct setup. The vertical accuracy of RTK is generally around 1-2 cm, but it can vary based on factors like satellite geometry, atmospheric conditions, signal obstructions, and the quality of the base station data. However, it's important to note that although RTK provides very precise relative measurements, the absolute accuracy can depend on the accuracy of the reference station coordinates.
What is the function of the Ditch Assist GPS cable when used with a John Deere smart antenna?The Ditch Assist GPS cable provides power to the John Deere smart antenna and directly extracts the necessary position information into the Ditch Assist Control Module.
How do I power the John Deere smart antenna when used with Ditch Assist?The John Deere smart antenna is powered by the Ditch Assist Control Module via the GPS cable.
Do I need to connect the GPS to the tractor or the GreenStar Display when using Ditch Assist?No, in this setup, there is no need to connect the GPS to the tractor or the GreenStar Display.
Where should I configure the John Deere smart antenna receiver when using Ditch Assist?The receiver should be configured while it is connected to the John Deere CAN system, such as when it is installed on the tractor's roof.
What should I do with the John Deere receiver after it has been properly configured for use with Ditch Assist?Once properly configured, you can remove the receiver and attach it to the implement, connecting it to the Ditch Assist Control Module using the appropriate cable.
How do I access the settings of the John Deere smart antenna?You access the settings of the John Deere smart antenna through the "Menu" button on your John Deere GreenStar display.
What should I look for in the GreenStar display to access the settings of the John Deere smart antenna?After pressing the "Menu" button, scroll to "StarFire Receiver" (or "StarFire 6000" or "StarFire 3000" etc., depending on your model), and select it.
How do I adjust the NMEA Strings for the John Deere smart antenna?Once inside the "StarFire Receiver" menu, scroll to "NMEA Strings" and select it. You will then see a list of NMEA strings such as GGA, VTG, etc. Toggle the strings you need to 'ON'.
Which NMEA Strings should be activated for use with Ditch Assist?For Ditch Assist, the GGA and VTG NMEA strings should be toggled to 'ON'.
What is the correct BAUD rate setting for John Deere smart antenna when used with Ditch Assist?When using John Deere smart antenna with Ditch Assist, the BAUD rate is typically set to 38,400.
How do I know if my John Deere smart antenna is properly configured for use with Ditch Assist?After making changes in the "StarFire Receiver" menu, exit the menu. Your receiver should now be broadcasting the selected NMEA strings, indicating that it is properly configured for use with Ditch Assist.
What does the Ditch Assist Control Module do?The Ditch Assist Control Module supplies power to the receiver and retrieves the required GPS messages from it.
Why is it necessary to connect the receiver to the John Deere CAN system for configuration?Connecting the receiver to the John Deere CAN system, such as when it's installed on the tractor's roof, allows for proper configuration.
What is the role of NMEA strings in the configuration of the John Deere smart antenna?NMEA strings like GGA and VTG are the necessary GPS messages that the Ditch Assist Control Module needs to extract from the antenna.
How do I know if the John Deere smart antenna is broadcasting the selected NMEA strings?After exiting the configuration menu on the GreenStar Display, your receiver should be broadcasting the selected NMEA strings. This means it's configured correctly.
What is the significance of the BAUD rate in the configuration of John Deere smart antenna?The BAUD rate ensures the correct speed of data transfer between the GPS receiver and the Ditch Assist Control Module. For Ditch Assist, it is typically set to 38,400.
What is the StarFire Receiver menu?The StarFire Receiver menu on the GreenStar Display is where you can access and adjust the settings for the John Deere smart antenna.
Is there a need to reconfigure the John Deere smart antenna if I move it from the tractor to the implement?No, once properly configured, you can remove the receiver from the tractor and attach it to the implement. The configuration will remain intact.
Can I power the John Deere smart antenna without the Ditch Assist Control Module?In this setup, the John Deere smart antenna receives its power from the Ditch Assist Control Module via the GPS cable, so it's necessary for powering the antenna.
How does the Ditch Assist Control Module connect to the John Deere smart antenna?The Ditch Assist Control Module connects to the John Deere smart antenna using the appropriate GPS cable.
Does the John Deere smart antenna broadcast the same NMEA strings regardless of the application?No, the NMEA strings to be broadcasted are selected based on the application. For Ditch Assist, GGA and VTG strings are typically used.
How do I change the BAUD rate on the John Deere smart antenna?The BAUD rate can be adjusted in the StarFire Receiver menu on the GreenStar Display after selecting the NMEA strings.
What happens if the BAUD rate is not set correctly on the John Deere smart antenna?If the BAUD rate isn't set correctly, it may cause data transfer issues between the GPS receiver and the Ditch Assist Control Module, leading to operational problems.
Where is the John Deere smart antenna typically installed when it is configured?When being configured, the John Deere smart antenna is typically installed on the tractor's roof where it connects to the John Deere CAN system.
How does the Ditch Assist system use the GPS messages from the John Deere smart antenna?The Ditch Assist system uses the GPS messages, specifically the GGA and VTG NMEA strings, to extract necessary position information for its operation.
what is the GPS message rate?The GPS message rate for GGA and VTG messages should be set to 5Hz or 10Hz depending on available options on your receiver
What is the purpose of the Ditch Assist GPS cable when using a John Deere smart antenna?The Ditch Assist GPS cable provides power to the smart antenna and directly extracts the necessary position information into the Control Module.
Do I need to connect the GPS to the tractor or the GreenStar Display in this setup?No, there is no need to connect the GPS to the tractor or the GreenStar Display when using a John Deere smart antenna with Ditch Assist.
When should the receiver be configured for John Deere smart antenna?The receiver should be configured while it is connected to the John Deere CAN system, such as when it is installed on the tractor's roof.
How can I connect the receiver to the Ditch Assist Control Module?Once properly configured, you can remove the receiver and attach it to the implement, connecting it to the Ditch Assist Control Module using the appropriate cable.
Who supplies power to the receiver in this setup?Ditch Assist supplies power to the receiver in this setup.
How can I access the "StarFire Receiver" menu?Press the "Menu" button on your John Deere GreenStar display and scroll to "StarFire Receiver", then select it.
Where can I find the "NMEA Strings" option?Once inside the "StarFire Receiver" menu, scroll to "NMEA Strings" and select it.
What are the NMEA strings needed for Ditch Assist?For Ditch Assist, the NMEA strings needed are GGA and VTG.
How can I enable the required NMEA strings?Toggle the required NMEA strings such as GGA and VTG to 'ON'.
What should be the message rate for the NMEA strings?The message rate for the NMEA strings should be set to either 5Hz or 10Hz.
What should be the BAUD rate setting for Ditch Assist?The BAUD rate should typically be set to 38,400 for Ditch Assist.
How will I know if the receiver is broadcasting the selected NMEA strings?Once the changes are complete, connect the receiver to the Ditch Assist Control Module and verify that the Status 2 light on the module begins to flash, which indicates that GPS data are being received.
Is the receiver connected directly to the tractor when using Ditch Assist?No, the receiver is not connected directly to the tractor when using Ditch Assist, it's connected to the Ditch Assist Control Module.
What is the role of the Ditch Assist Control Module in this setup?The Ditch Assist Control Module retrieves the required GPS messages from the receiver and also supplies power to the receiver.
How do I exit the menu after making changes to the NMEA strings?After making changes to the NMEA strings, you can exit the menu simply by pressing the "Exit" or "Back" button on your display.
Which models of the StarFire Receiver can I use for this setup?You can use different models of the StarFire Receiver such as StarFire 6000 or StarFire 3000, depending on your equipment.
Can I alter the BAUD rate other than 38,400 for Ditch Assist?Typically, the BAUD rate is set to 38,400 for Ditch Assist. However, you should refer to your specific model's manual or consult with a technician for any adjustments.
How do I confirm the successful configuration of the receiver?After configuring the receiver, connect it to the Ditch Assist Control Module. If the Status 2 light on the module begins to flash, it indicates successful configuration as GPS data are being received.
Can I configure the receiver while it is not connected to the John Deere CAN system?The receiver should be configured while it is connected to the John Deere CAN system for proper setup.
What happens if I do not set the required NMEA strings to 'ON'?If you do not set the required NMEA strings to 'ON', the receiver may not broadcast the necessary GPS data for Ditch Assist to function properly.
Can the message rate for the NMEA strings be set to other than 5Hz or 10Hz?For optimal operation, the message rate for the NMEA strings should be set to either 5Hz or 10Hz. Other settings should be confirmed with your equipment's manual or a technical expert.
Can the receiver work without being connected to the Ditch Assist Control Module?The receiver needs to be connected to the Ditch Assist Control Module in this setup to get power and send the necessary GPS messages.
What should I do if the Status 2 light on the module does not begin to flash?If the Status 2 light on the module does not begin to flash after you've connected the receiver, check your settings and connections to ensure they're correct. If problems persist, consult with a technician.
What types of NMEA strings are generally displayed in the menu?In the menu, you should see a list of NMEA strings such as GGA, VTG, etc.
Where does the receiver typically get installed for configuration?The receiver typically gets installed on the tractor's roof for configuration.
What do the Status 2 and Status 1 lights indicate?The Status 2 and Status 1 lights flash when a valid RS232 or CAN based GPS message packet is respectively received.
What do the Status 2 and Status 1 lights help diagnose?These lights serve as an effective diagnostic tool for GPS connectivity issues.
What does it mean if the Status 2 or Status 1 light is flashing when a GPS device is connected?If a GPS device is connected and the light is flashing, this is indicative of successful receipt of a valid GPS message.
What does it signify if the Status 2 or Status 1 light is not flashing?If the light isn't flashing, this signifies that no GPS messages are being received.
What information does the frequency of the flashing lights convey?The frequency of the flashing lights conveys information about the message rate of the GPS device.
What does a slow flashing rate or 1 flash per second indicate?A slow flashing rate, or 1 flash per second, suggests that the GPS device may be outputting messages at only 1Hz.
How can the message rate be improved for the GPS device?To improve efficiency, it is advisable to adjust the GPS device to output at a rate of 5Hz or 10Hz.
What will happen to the Status LEDs on the Control Module if the GPS device outputs at a rate of 5Hz or 10Hz?If the GPS device is adjusted to output at a rate of 5Hz or 10Hz, the Status LEDs on the Control Module will flash more rapidly.
Is the Status 2 light an indicator for both RS232 and CAN based GPS message packets?No, the Status 2 light flashes specifically when a valid RS232 GPS message packet is received.
What happens when a valid CAN based GPS message packet is received?When a valid CAN based GPS message packet is received, the Status 1 light flashes.
Is it a good sign if the Status 2 or Status 1 light is flashing?Yes, if the Status 2 or Status 1 light is flashing, it indicates that a valid GPS message is being received.
What could potentially be the issue if none of the Status lights are flashing?If none of the Status lights are flashing, it could mean that no GPS messages are being received, indicating a potential connectivity issue.
What can the Status lights diagnose apart from GPS connectivity issues?The Status lights primarily serve as a diagnostic tool for GPS connectivity issues, and they also provide information on the message rate from the GPS device.
Does a slow rate of flashing mean that the GPS device is faulty?A slow rate of flashing does not necessarily mean the GPS device is faulty. It could suggest that the device is outputting messages at a slower rate, such as 1Hz.
What can be inferred from the Status LEDs on the Control Module flashing rapidly?Rapid flashing of the Status LEDs on the Control Module suggests that the GPS device is outputting messages at a higher rate, likely 5Hz or 10Hz.
Can the output rate of the GPS device be adjusted while it's connected?Yes, the output rate of the GPS device can usually be adjusted while it's connected. However, you should refer to the specific device manual for accurate instructions.
Do both Status lights need to flash for the GPS to function correctly?Typically, the Status 2 light flashes for RS232 GPS messages and the Status 1 light for CAN based GPS messages. They don't necessarily have to flash simultaneously, as it depends on the type of message packet received.
What should be the minimum output rate for efficient GPS functioning?For efficient functioning of the GPS device, the output rate should be at least 5Hz or 10Hz.
Can the flashing of the Status lights indicate issues other than GPS connectivity?While the primary function of the Status lights is to indicate GPS connectivity and message rate, any other unusual patterns may also indicate different issues and should be checked with the device manual or a technician.
If the Status lights are not flashing, does it mean the Control Module is not receiving power?Not necessarily. While a lack of flashing Status lights could indicate that no GPS messages are being received, it does not inherently mean that the Control Module isn't receiving power. Other diagnostic methods should be employed to confirm.
How do I adjust the output rate of the GPS device?The method of adjusting the output rate of the GPS device would depend on the specific device model. Refer to the device's manual for detailed instructions.
What is a valid GPS message?A valid GPS message refers to the correct and usable data sent by the GPS device. It includes information needed for navigation and tracking.
What does a CAN based GPS message packet indicate?A CAN based GPS message packet indicates that the GPS data is being communicated via the Controller Area Network (CAN) system, which is commonly used in automation and vehicles.
Can the frequency of Status light flashes indicate the GPS device's model or type?No, the frequency of Status light flashes mainly indicates the message rate of the GPS device, not its model or type.
Is the rapid flashing of the Status LEDs on the Control Module a cause for concern?No, rapid flashing of the Status LEDs on the Control Module is not a cause for concern. It indicates that the GPS device is outputting messages at a higher rate, which is beneficial for more efficient operation.
How can I verify if my GPS is working?You can verify if your GPS is working by checking whether either the Status 1 or Status 2 light on the Ditch Assist Control Module are flashing. You should also run the app and connect the tablet to the control module to check if valid GPS GNSS information is shown in the GNSS panel.
What does it mean if the Status 1 or Status 2 light on the Ditch Assist Control Module is flashing?If either the Status 1 or Status 2 light on the Ditch Assist Control Module is flashing, this indicates that a valid GPS message is being received, suggesting that the GPS is working.
How can I check the GPS GNSS information?You can check the GPS GNSS information by running the Ditch Assist app and connecting your tablet to the control module. The GNSS panel in the app should display the GPS GNSS information.
What could be the problem if lights are flashing on the module but no information shows up in the app?If the lights on the module are flashing but no information shows up in the app, this could indicate that your GPS message settings or baud rate may be incorrect.
Can the flashing of Status 1 or Status 2 light ensure the correct functioning of the GPS?While the flashing of Status 1 or Status 2 light can indicate that the GPS is receiving valid messages, it is also important to verify the GPS GNSS information in the app for complete assurance.
How can I rectify if there is no information showing up in the app despite the module lights flashing?If there's no information showing up in the app despite the module lights flashing, it may be due to incorrect GPS message settings or baud rate. You may need to check and adjust these settings as per your device manual.
Is it enough to check only the GNSS panel for verifying GPS functionality?Checking the GNSS panel in the app is an important part of verifying GPS functionality. However, it should be coupled with checking the Status 1 and Status 2 lights on the Control Module for comprehensive assurance.
What does the GNSS panel display in the app?The GNSS panel in the app displays the GPS GNSS information, which includes data necessary for navigation and tracking.
What does it mean if the module lights are not flashing?If the module lights are not flashing, it could indicate that no GPS messages are being received, which may suggest a connectivity issue or a problem with the GPS.
What is the role of the Ditch Assist app in verifying GPS functionality?The Ditch Assist app helps in verifying GPS functionality by displaying valid GPS GNSS information when connected to the Control Module.
What could be the reasons for incorrect GPS message settings or baud rate?Incorrect GPS message settings or baud rate could result from incorrect configuration or manual adjustments. It is advisable to refer to your device manual or consult a technician to correct these settings.
Does the tablet need to be connected to the Control Module for checking GPS functionality?Yes, for checking GPS GNSS information, you should connect the tablet to the Control Module and run the Ditch Assist app.
Can incorrect baud rate affect the GPS functionality?Yes, an incorrect baud rate can impact the communication between your GPS device and the Control Module, thereby affecting the GPS functionality.
Can I check GPS functionality without the Ditch Assist app?While the Ditch Assist app is a key tool for verifying GPS functionality, the flashing of Status 1 or Status 2 light on the Control Module can also provide an indication of whether the GPS is receiving valid messages. However, for a comprehensive check, it is advisable to use the app.
Can I manually adjust the GPS message settings or baud rate?Yes, you can manually adjust the GPS message settings or baud rate. However, make sure to follow the device manual to make the correct adjustments.
How often should I verify the GPS functionality?The frequency for verifying GPS functionality depends on the usage and operational requirements. However, it is good practice to check it periodically and whenever you experience issues with GPS operations.
What does the baud rate mean in GPS settings?The baud rate in GPS settings refers to the speed at which data is transmitted between the GPS device and the Control Module. It can impact the performance and accuracy of the GPS.
What should I do if I see an error in the GPS GNSS information on the app?If you see an error in the GPS GNSS information on the app, try checking your GPS message settings or baud rate. If issues persist, it is advisable to consult a technician or refer to your device manual.
Can I troubleshoot GPS issues on my own?Yes, basic troubleshooting like checking the Status 1 or Status 2 lights on the Control Module and verifying GPS GNSS information on the app can be done on your own. However, for more complex issues, it might be best to consult a technician.
What does GNSS stand for in GPS settings?GNSS stands for Global Navigation Satellite System. It is a system that involves a network of satellites providing signals from space transmitting positioning and timing data to GNSS receivers.
How can I enable NMEA messages on Trimble/Case-IH GPS receivers?You can enable NMEA messages on Trimble/Case-IH GPS receivers by accessing the GPS receiver's settings or configuration menu, locating the NMEA messages settings within the communication settings, and enabling the desired NMEA messages.
Where can I access the GPS receiver's settings or configuration menu on a Trimble/Case-IH GPS receiver?The GPS receiver's settings or configuration menu can typically be accessed through the display unit or the receiver's interface.
What options should I look for in the Trimble/Case-IH GPS receiver's settings?In the receiver's settings, you should look for the section or option related to communication or output settings. Within these, you'll find the NMEA messages settings or options.
What NMEA messages can be enabled on a Trimble/Case-IH GPS receiver?Trimble/Case-IH GPS receivers usually provide options to enable specific NMEA messages, such as GGA, VTG, or others.
How can I adjust the message rate on a Trimble/Case-IH GPS receiver?If available, you can adjust the message rate on Trimble/Case-IH GPS receivers by selecting the appropriate option within the NMEA messages settings. This allows you to specify the rate at which the NMEA messages are output (e.g., 10Hz or 5Hz).
What should I do after making changes in the Trimble/Case-IH GPS receiver settings?After making changes in the Trimble/Case-IH GPS receiver settings, you should save the changes and exit the settings menu.
What are NMEA messages in the context of GPS receivers?NMEA messages refer to a standard data format that is used by GPS and mapping devices, including Trimble/Case-IH GPS receivers. These messages contain information such as location, velocity, and other relevant data.
What does a message rate of 10Hz or 5Hz mean?A message rate of 10Hz or 5Hz specifies the frequency at which the NMEA messages are sent by the GPS receiver. For example, 10Hz means ten messages per second and 5Hz means five messages per second.
What are the benefits of enabling specific NMEA messages?Enabling specific NMEA messages allows you to customize the data that your Trimble/Case-IH GPS receiver sends. This can improve efficiency by only transmitting the data that is needed for your specific application.
How do I know which NMEA messages to enable on my Trimble/Case-IH GPS receiver?The NMEA messages to enable depend on your specific needs and the requirements of any connected systems or software. It's best to consult your system documentation or a technical specialist to understand which messages are necessary for your operation.
Can I change the NMEA messages settings while the Trimble/Case-IH GPS receiver is in use?It is advisable to change the NMEA messages settings when the GPS receiver is not actively in use to avoid disrupting any ongoing tasks or data transmission.
Can the Trimble/Case-IH GPS receiver save multiple configurations?This depends on the specific model of the Trimble/Case-IH GPS receiver. Some models might allow multiple configurations, while others may not. Please refer to the user manual for specific information.
How do the NMEA messages settings impact the performance of the Trimble/Case-IH GPS receiver?The NMEA messages settings can impact the speed and efficiency of data transmission from the GPS receiver. Adjusting the settings to match the requirements of your operation can optimize the receiver's performance.
What if I can't find the communication or output settings on my Trimble/Case-IH GPS receiver?If you can't locate the communication or output settings on your Trimble/Case-IH GPS receiver, refer to the device manual or contact the manufacturer's customer support for assistance.
Can I disable certain NMEA messages on the Trimble/Case-IH GPS receiver?Yes, you can disable certain NMEA messages on the Trimble/Case-IH GPS receiver. This can be done in the NMEA messages settings or options within the communication settings.
]]> Ditch Assist Installation Instructions https://www.ditchassist.com/ditch-assist-installation-instructions/ Tue, 06 Jun 2023 23:12:34 +0000 https://www.ditchassist.com/?p=209236 Installation Instructions

Depending upon the type of equipment you plan to install Ditch Assist on, the exact method will vary somewhat. Ditch Assist is extremely simple to install and move between equipment. The following guidelines should help you determine a suitable installation for your particular equipment.

IMPORTANT: Make sure that Ditch Assist components are installed so that parts, cables and hoses will not be stretched, pinched, or otherwise damaged during the operation of equipment. Keep in mind that cables and hoses may be subject to stretching when the implement is turning, and that components mounted near the hitch or rear of the tractor may come into contact with other parts when cornering.

Proportional Valve Setup

Consult the specific installation instructions that should be included in your installation kit - if these are missing please contact your dealer to obtain them. Ensure you assemble the hydraulic components as shown. Below are common hydraulic assembly instructions for our Bucher and Rexroth valves (other valves may be slightly different):

For the Bucher valve, the automation hoses necessary for constant flow should be connected to the external valve block (the block that has the dial). Meanwhile, the fittings for the manual bypass and implement hoses should be attached to the central block, which is situated between the solenoids.

For the Rexroth valve, the hydraulic hoses needed for automation (providing a constant flow) should be connected to the ports located on the valve block's top surface. Meanwhile, the implement connectors and manual bypass fittings should be attached to the ports situated on the valve's side.

Control Module & Valve Installation

Ensure that the Control Module is securely installed either at the implement's front, near the hitch, or at the tractor's rear, with minimal obstructions to the line of sight to the cab. The included magnetic mount can be used for this purpose, or you can fabricate your own if needed. The Control Module can be installed in either a horizontal or vertical orientation.

IMPORTANT: While the Control Module is sealed against weather, and all connectors that plug into it come with waterproof gaskets, we strongly advise installing it with the ports facing downward to prevent water from accumulating in and around them. If the wiring harness connectors are removed from the Control Module, this might allow moisture to infiltrate. Always ensure all port connectors or covers are in place when leaving the Control Module outside.

Position the proportional valve in a place that allows the implement hydraulic hoses to connect to it, and enables the included hoses you attached to the valve to reach the remote ports at the tractor's rear. To prevent damage during operation, the valve must be securely installed. If the provided angle iron mount is used, the pre-drilled holes will align with the valve's mounting holes, and the mounting plate should be either bolted or welded to the equipment.

SUGGESTION: If you intend to use Ditch Assist with multiple different implements, consider permanently mounting the valve on your tractor, as it may be more practical.

Example Mounting Setup on a Scraper or Land Leveler

Valve bracket should be mounted securely to the implement. Do not weld the bracket with the valve mounted. Remove the module before welding anything to the implement. Make sure the module is mounted where it will not be dislodged by debris, moving parts, or hydraulic lines. Secure all cables and hydraulic lines so they will not be pinched, stretched or damaged during operation.

Connecting the Hydraulic Hoses

The two hoses coming from the P and T ports on the valve, in addition to the hoses for the bypass, need to be connected to the tractor - a total of four hoses. The hydraulic remotes on the tractor that Ditch Assist is connected to should be set to a continuous flow, allowing the valve to raise and lower the implement as required. Depending on the age and size of your tractor, you might need to adjust the flow rate if you observe that the implement is moving up or down too quickly or too slowly. The App also contains settings for valve response - refer to the Settings Menu for more details.

Connecting the Wiring Harness to the Valve

Attach the two plugs from the harness to the connectors on the proportional valve. You might need to swap the connectors later if you notice that the manual up/down controls in the App are functioning in reverse. Specifically, if manually lowering the implement in the App causes it to lift, you'll need to switch the wired connectors on the valve. Refer to the Quick Start guide at the beginning of the manual to understand the process for determining this.

Wiring Harness Installation

The two large Deutsch connectors on the main harness should be plugged into ports A and C of the Ditch Assist Control Module. Each connector has a distinct 'key', ensuring that it's impossible to make incorrect connections. Ports B and D should be left vacant and sealed with the included dust plugs for protection.

Link the power harness directly to the tractor battery terminals and route the cable back to the main harness. Connect it to the power connector on the main harness (it can only fit into one connector, so it's not possible to make an incorrect connection). Make sure to connect the power harness exclusively to the vehicle battery. One of the most frequent troubleshooting issues we encounter is due to the power coming from a trailer plug or an in-cab power strip, which either cannot supply sufficient current to the solenoids or is prone to power surges that cause problems with the microcontrollers.

Finally, connect the GPS breakout cable to the remaining open connector on the harness and route it to the GPS antenna location.

Any slack or excess cables should be secured using ties or other suitable means to prevent them from becoming damaged during machine operation.

GPS Information

Ditch Assist was developed with compatibility in mind, designed to work with a wide range of GPS brands and models. We support both the NMEA 2000 message standard, which is a CAN-based communication protocol, as well as the NMEA 0183, a standard associated with 'serial port' communication.

GPS Requirements and Settings for Ditch Assist

NMEA Output Settings Required

You should configure your GPS to emit the following NMEA messages:

  • SET BAUD RATE TO 38,400
  • GGA MESSAGE AT 10Hz (5Hz if 10Hz is not available)
  • VTG MESSAGE AT 10Hz (5Hz if 10Hz is not available)
  • DISABLE ALL OTHER MESSAGES

(If you can't designate a specific message rate for each message, then enable the highest rate for all messages.)

Diagnosing GPS/GNSS Connectivity using the Status LEDs on the Control Module

The Status 2 and Status 1 lights act as indicators when a valid RS232 or CAN based GPS message packet is respectively received. These lights serve as an effective diagnostic tool for GPS connectivity issues. If a GPS device is connected and the light is flashing, this is indicative of successful receipt of a valid GPS message. On the other hand, if the light isn't flashing, this signifies that no GPS messages are being received. Furthermore, the frequency of the flashing lights conveys information about the message rate. For instance, a slow flashing rate, or 1 flash per second, suggests that the GPS device may be outputting messages at only 1Hz. To improve efficiency, it is advisable to adjust the device to output at a rate of 5Hz or 10Hz, which will correspondingly cause the Status LEDs on the Control Module to flash more rapidly.

How to Configure John Deere Smart Antennas

When utilizing a John Deere smart antenna with Ditch Assist, the Ditch Assist GPS cable will provide power to the unit and directly extract the necessary position information into the Control Module. In this setup, there is no need to connect the GPS to the tractor or the GreenStar Display. It is essential to configure the receiver while it is connected to the John Deere CAN system, such as when it is installed on the tractor's roof. Once properly configured, you can remove the receiver and attach it to the implement, connecting it to the Ditch Assist Control Module using the appropriate cable. Ditch Assist will supply power to the receiver and retrieve the required GPS messages from it.

  1. Press the "Menu" button on your John Deere GreenStar display.
  2. Scroll to "StarFire Receiver" (or "StarFire 6000" or "StarFire 3000" etc., depending on your model), and select it.
  3. Once inside the "StarFire Receiver" menu, scroll to "NMEA Strings" and select it.
  4. You will see a list of NMEA strings such as GGA, VTG, etc. Toggle the strings you need (for Ditch Assist, GGA and VTG) to 'ON'. 
  5. Set the message rate to either 5Hz or 10Hz
  6. Also, ensure that the BAUD rate is set correctly, typically to 38,400 for Ditch Assist.
  7. Exit the menu once the changes are complete. 
  8. Your receiver should now be broadcasting the selected NMEA strings. Connect it to the Ditch Assist Control Module and verify that the Status 2 light on the module begins to flash as this indicates that GPS data are being received

How to Configure Trimble/Case IH GNSS Receivers 

To enable NMEA messages on Trimble/Case-IH GPS receivers, you can follow these general steps:

1. Access the GPS receiver's settings or configuration menu. This can typically be done through the display unit or the receiver's interface.

2. Look for the section or option related to communication or output settings.

3. Locate the NMEA messages settings or options within the communication settings.

4. Enable or activate the desired NMEA messages. Trimble/Case-IH GPS receivers usually provide options to enable specific NMEA messages, such as GGA, VTG, or others.

5. Adjust the message rate if available. Some receivers allow you to specify the rate at which the NMEA messages are output (e.g., 10Hz or 5Hz).

6. Save the changes and exit the settings menu.

How to Configure Emlid RS2 Receivers

To enable NMEA messages on the Emlid RS2 GNSS receiver, you can follow these steps:

1. Connect to the RS2 GNSS receiver using the ReachView app on your mobile device or web browser.

2. Open the ReachView app and navigate to the "GNSS Settings" or "RTK Settings" section.

3. Look for the option related to NMEA messages or NMEA output. The specific location may vary depending on the version of ReachView, but it is usually found within the GNSS or RTK settings.

4. Enable or toggle the NMEA messages option. You have the option to select specific NMEA message types or configure the message rate.

5. Set the baud rate to 38,400 

6. Save the changes or apply the settings.

7. The RS2 GNSS receiver will now output the selected NMEA messages according to the configured settings.

How to Configure Outback GPS Receivers

Many Outback GPS smart antennas are plug-and-play and will provide the required GPS messages over CAN bus automatically when connected to Ditch Assist with our Outback GPS harness. If your Outback antenna has a small circular connector then it likely falls into this category. Otherwise, to manually enable NMEA messages on Outback GPS receivers, you can follow these general steps:

1. Access the GPS receiver's menu or settings. This is typically done through a display or user interface on the device.

2. Look for a section related to communication settings or NMEA output configuration.

3. Within the communication settings, you should find an option to enable NMEA messages or configure the NMEA output.

4. Enable the NMEA output or select the desired NMEA message types you want to receive.

5. Check if there are additional settings to adjust the baud rate and message rate (such as 10Hz or 5Hz).

6. Save the changes and exit the menu or settings.

Please note that specific steps may vary depending on the model and firmware version of your Outback GPS receiver. It is recommended to refer to the user manual or consult the manufacturer's documentation for precise instructions related to your specific model.

GPS Corrections and Accuracy

Ditch Assist relies on a precise GPS signal for optimal performance. If your GPS lacks accuracy, it may impact the performance of Ditch Assist.

IMPORTANT: If you are not using RTK with your own base station, we highly recommend using a subscription-based correction service with a published vertical accuracy of at worst 2-3 inches as a minimum requirement. It is possible that you may find the GPS accuracy provided by your current system to be insufficient. This is not a fault of Ditch Assist, as it simply processes the incoming GPS data it receives.

Ditch Assist is not compatible with low-accuracy correction signals like WAAS or low-accuracy SBAS corrections such as OmniSTAR L1. We recommend, at a minimum, utilizing a subscription-based dual-frequency (L1/L2) satellite-delivered correction signal.

Below are specifications for the recommended GPS corrections:

USE WITH CAUTION:

Subscription-based L1/L2 correction services like CenterPoint RTX, TerraStar, or Atlas. Expect slight fluctuations in elevation readings with these systems (e.g., the machine may experience minor up/down movements even when stationary). Not recommended for use in pancake-flat fields.

SHOULD BE OKAY UNDER MOST CIRCUMSTANCES:

Network RTK Correction through CORS, Cellular, or Long Range Radio link to a remote base station. These corrections typically provide good accuracy for surface grading. Keep in mind that the closer you are to the base station, the better the repeatable accuracy will be. Being near a cell tower does not necessarily mean you are close to the base station. If you don't experience issues with signal dropouts or loss of accuracy, this is a good option.

BEST OPTION FOR RELIABILITY AND ACCURACY:

RTK using your own base station within line-of-sight offers the highest level of accuracy. By setting up your own base station, you can determine the distance and adjust its position if necessary. For optimum accuracy, ensure that you are within 2 miles of the base station. With this type of RTK setup, you should experience minimal fluctuation in elevation readings while stationary, and the implement's hydraulic movements due to RTK fluctuations should be nearly imperceptible.

Please note that while we do not endorse using any correction less accurate than RTK, some users have chosen to use lower accuracy systems and have been satisfied with the performance.

Mounting the GPS Antenna

IMPORTANT: The GPS antenna to be used with Ditch Assist must be mounted on the implement to be controlled and must be attached to a part of the implement that moves up and down in proportion to the cutting edge or blade. Failure to adhere to this principle will result in incorrect operation.

The GPS antenna should also be mounted in a raised position so that it sits above any objects that may cause the satellite signal to be blocked or deflected. Incoming GPS signals that bounce off a metal object near the GPS antenna may contribute to decreased GPS accuracy, so it is important to ensure the GPS antenna is mounted away from any such objects. Also keep in mind that the working position may be considerably lower than the transport position, so a taller mast may be required.

Mounting Your Android Device

The Ditch Assist Kit includes a RAM mount designed for 10” tablets. Use the mount to securely install your device in the tractor cab somewhere you can easily see and tap the screen. You may also wish to power your device to avoid battery drain on your device. (Tablet & charge cables are not included in the standard Ditch Assist kit).

]]> Understanding How Ditch Assist Works https://www.ditchassist.com/understanding-how-ditch-assist-works/ Tue, 06 Jun 2023 23:11:08 +0000 https://www.ditchassist.com/?p=209232 Understanding How Ditch Assist Works
  1. Ditch Assist's primary function is to sustain a target elevation with the implement blade. This target elevation can be derived from one of the following: a 'best-fit' design generated through Slope-IQ, which is based on a survey and operator input; a pre-established land leveling design created in third-party software and loaded into the Ditch Assist app; or a straightforward target grounded on a specified elevation (for instance, when leveling an area to a particular elevation), or on a grade plane depending on a starting elevation.
  2. The Ditch Assist Control Module obtains GPS location data from the GPS system. This information is then wirelessly transmitted to the Android device that is operating the Ditch Assist App.
  1. Based on the current location, the Ditch Assist app determines the target elevation for that point. It then conveys this information to the Control Module.
  2. The Control Module compares the intended elevation with the actual one, thus establishing the required response - that is, deciding whether the implement needs to be elevated or lowered to achieve the desired level. This adjustment is initiated by activating the solenoids of the PWM valve, leading to vertical movement. Due to the continuous motion of the vehicle and implement, coupled with minor fluctuations even with RTK, precisely attaining the target is almost unachievable. This results in a constant cycle of minute adjustments, fluctuating between raising and lowering, in an effort to reach the target. While subtle movements might go unnoticed, more significant variances will lead to noticeable changes in the hydraulic cylinders' positions to modify the implement's height.

Important GNSS (GPS) Limitations to Understand

Ditch Assist heavily relies on precise GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System) information. A lack of high-accuracy positioning data can result in less than optimal performance. 

1. Non-RTK GNSS: Standard GNSS systems like GPS, without the added precision of Real-Time Kinematics (RTK), can have position errors up to several meters. For a system like Ditch Assist, which requires centimeter-level precision, this can lead to inaccuracies in the alignment and leveling of the land. For instance, a non-RTK GNSS might cause the implement to be moved when it's not necessary, or it might fail to signal when the implement does need to be moved, leading to uneven terrain.

2. Performance Issues with RTK: While RTK provides more precise positioning data than standard GNSS, it isn't immune to performance issues. RTK requires a stable communication link between the base station and the receiver, and disruptions to this link can result in temporary loss of RTK's centimeter-level precision. For instance, if the radio link fails or the base station loses its own GNSS signal, the receiver might switch back to standard GNSS accuracy until the issue is resolved. This can result in similar inaccuracies to those experienced with non-RTK GNSS.

3. Multipath Errors: Both non-RTK GNSS and RTK can be affected by multipath errors, which occur when satellite signals are reflected off nearby objects before reaching the receiver. These reflected signals can confuse the receiver, leading to incorrect position calculations. In the context of Ditch Assist, this could mean incorrect implement adjustments.

4. GNSS Signal Blockage: This can be a problem in areas with tall trees, buildings, or other obstacles that might block the line of sight to the GNSS satellites. The loss of signals from some satellites can decrease the accuracy of the position calculations. For Ditch Assist, this could again lead to errors in implement movement.

The use of RTK GNSS technology can mitigate some of these issues, providing higher accuracy for precision agriculture applications like Ditch Assist. However, users should still be aware of potential sources of error and work to minimize them for the best performance.

]]> Ditch Assist Operating Principles https://www.ditchassist.com/ditch-assist-operating-principles/ Tue, 06 Jun 2023 23:04:44 +0000 https://www.ditchassist.com/?p=209223 Ditch Assist Operating Principles

The Ditch Assist Control Module is the central component of the Ditch Assist system

The Control Module establishes its own WiFi access point when it is powered on

The user operates Ditch Assist via the Ditch Assist Android App that is installed on an Android tablet

The tablet and Control Module communicate wirelessly via WiFi

Ditch Assist uses the data fed from a RTK GNSS receiver to automatically adjust the implement height via a PWM hydraulic valve

Typical Installation

The Ditch Assist Control Module and proportional valve (if used) are mounted either near the hitch on the implement being controlled, or at the rear of the tractor. The power harness is connected directly to the tractor battery. The GPS antenna is mounted in a raised position on the implement being controlled, and is mounted to a location that moves up and down proportionally to the cutting edge.

A common setup for a land leveler or similar implement with a straight blade involves mounting the PWM valve on the implement tongue, a few feet away from the hitch point. The Ditch Assist Control Module is attached to the tongue or any other suitable spot, using either the magnetic mounts or a custom-made bracket. The GNSS receiver is placed at the top of the implement. Please note that you may need a raised mount if the implement has components that overhang or for shorter implements that might obstruct the GNSS's view of the sky due to the tractor.

In a typical configuration for a pull-type scraper, the valve is installed on the tongue using either the provided or a custom bracket, with the Ditch Assist Control Module also mounted on the tongue. Please note that in this specific setup, if there are any WiFi connectivity issues, the Ditch Assist Control Module can alternatively be installed on the vertical part of the scraper frame (for instance, near the white label), as this would provide a better line of sight to the tractor cab.

For a scraper, it's essential that the GNSS is mounted in a manner that allows it to move proportionately to the cutting edge. This generally necessitates a mast to keep it above the metal components surrounding it when the scraper is in its maximum lowered position.

]]>
